nativeRange.habitat: Muddy estuaries, mangrove margins, and brackish tidal zones of Amazonian and Orinoco river systems; also penetrates freshwater rivers and streams; benthic ambush predator resting motionless on soft substrate or concealed beneath debris

decorPreferences: thick sand or fine-gravel substrate for resting
PVC pipes, clay caves, or hollow logs as hides
overhanging flat rocks for ambush concealment
low-light conditions to encourage daytime activity
minimal flow — sluggish or near-still water preferred
tight-fitting lid (escape risk when active at night)

diet.notes: Obligate live-feeder in most captive reports; staple prey includes live feeder fish (goldfish, rosy reds, guppies), live earthworms, live crayfish, and live shrimp. Some specimens can be weaned slowly onto freshly-killed or frozen-thawed fish and prawns via feeding tongs, but acceptance is inconsistent. Feeding tongs strongly recommended — venomous dorsal and opercular spines pose serious envenomation risk during hand-feeding. Feed every 3–5 days; overfeeding causes rapid obesity. Refuses prepared pellets and dried foods in virtually all hobbyist accounts.

avoidWith: all other fish (gape-limited predator; will consume anything that fits in mouth)
conspecifics (intense territorial aggression; severe bite wounds reported)
any tankmate small enough to be swallowed whole

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Batrachoides surinamensis; check photographer credit on FishBase page before use.

summary: Despite its trade name, the Freshwater Lionfish is a venomous Amazonian toadfish (family Batrachoididae), wholly unrelated to true lionfishes (Pterois, Scorpaenidae). A bottom-dwelling ambush predator reaching 40+ cm with venomous dorsal and opercular spines; euryhaline from freshwater into brackish estuaries. Must be kept alone; live-feeder dependent in most cases. Specialist-only species.
careNotes:
The Freshwater Lionfish is a misnomer: this species belongs to Batrachoididae (toadfishes), not Scorpaenidae, and has no close relationship to true lionfishes. Its popular name derives from a superficial spiny appearance. Batrachoides surinamensis is a stocky, dorsally-flattened benthic ambush predator native to Amazonian and Guyanese estuaries. Wild specimens tolerate a wide salinity range — from near-fresh river water up to full brackish estuaries — making them a genuine euryhaline species. In captivity, maintaining a slight brackish component (SG 1.002–1.010) is widely recommended to reduce osmoregulatory stress and improve long-term health, though the fish will survive in dechlorinated freshwater.

HANDLING WARNING: Batrachoides surinamensis possesses both hollow venomous dorsal fin spines and venomous opercular (gill-cover) spines. Envenomation causes immediate intense pain, localised tissue necrosis, and in severe cases systemic effects. Never handle bare-handed. Use thick rubber gloves, net, or a container when moving the fish. Keep a first-aid plan available; seek medical attention for any puncture wound.

Tank requirements: A minimum 400 L tank (150 cm footprint) is needed for a single adult. The substrate should be 5+ cm of soft sand — the fish rests on the bottom for extended periods and can partially bury. Provide multiple caves and overhangs (PVC pipes, clay pots, flat rocks). Lighting should be dim; the species is nocturnal and will refuse food under bright illumination. Water movement should be minimal. A tight-fitting, weighted lid is essential — the fish may attempt escape, particularly at night.

Feeding: Primarily live-feeder dependent. Feeder goldfish and rosy reds are convenient but nutritionally poor long-term; rotate with live earthworms, freshwater crayfish, and live shrimp. Some individuals can be weaned to frozen-thawed items via tongs over several weeks. Feed every 3–5 days; the species has a slow metabolism and is easily over-fed. Starvation tolerance is high — adults can fast for several weeks without harm.

Vocalisation: This species is a sonic toadfish; males produce a distinctive boat-whistle or fog-horn grunt using specialised swim-bladder muscles. The sound is audible outside the tank and is associated with territorial display and spawning readiness.

Note on taxonomy and trade names: The fish is sold as 'Freshwater Lionfish' and occasionally 'Freshwater Scorpionfish' in the ornamental trade, creating confusion with true lionfishes (Pterois spp., family Scorpaenidae). All three common names apply to the same species: Batrachoides surinamensis, family Batrachoididae.
breedingNotes: No captive breeding records are widely documented in the aquarium hobby. In the wild, toadfishes (Batrachoididae) are benthic, demersal spawners: males guard egg clutches deposited in sheltered cavities (under rocks, in burrows) and fan and clean the eggs until hatching. Larvae are large and lecithotrophic (yolk-sac dependent) at hatching. Replicating wild conditions (brackish water, suitable spawning cave, undisturbed pair) would likely be necessary for any captive breeding attempt, but sexing is difficult and conspecific aggression is severe enough to require careful supervision of any pairing attempt.

primary.name: FishBase — Batrachoides surinamensis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided taxonomy (Batrachoididae, Batrachoidiformes), native range (Suriname, Brazil, Trinidad, Venezuela, Guyana, French Guiana), adult TL to 45 cm, temperature range, habitat (muddy estuaries and freshwater rivers), diet (carnivore; fish and invertebrates), and synonymy. Confirmed euryhaline, benthic, and venomous-spine classification.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Batrachoides surinamensis
additional.notes: Provided detailed husbandry: minimum tank 400 L, substrate needs, brackish tolerance (SG up to 1.010), live-feeder diet dependency, venomous spine envenomation risk (dorsal and opercular), nocturnal behaviour, vocalisation via swim-bladder, and single-specimen keeping requirement. Key source for careNotes.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Toadfish Profile
additional.notes: Trade publication profile; confirmed common name confusion (Freshwater Lionfish vs true Pterois lionfishes), ambush predator behaviour, feeding strategy, brackish preference, and size reaching 40+ cm. Reinforced specialist-only recommendation.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Venomous and Dangerous Aquarium Fish
additional.notes: Consulted for general venomous-spine handling guidance applicable to Batrachoididae; confirmed that opercular spines in toadfishes are venomous and that envenomation warrants medical attention. No dedicated Batrachoides surinamensis page exists on Aquarium Co-Op.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Freshwater Toadfishes in the Aquarium
additional.notes: Historical hobbyist account of Batrachoididae in captivity; confirmed vocalisation behaviour (swim-bladder sonic muscles), feeding challenges with live-only diet, and difficulty of conspecific housing. Supplementary source for breedingNotes and vocalisation detail.

fish.breedingNotes: No documented captive breeding in the hobby. Wild Batrachoididae are benthic demersal spawners with paternal egg-guarding; replication in captivity would require brackish conditions, compatible pair, and spawning cave. Conspecific aggression makes pairing extremely risky.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Not actually a lionfish (Batrachoididae toadfish family). Brackish-tolerant ambush predator reaching 40+cm with VENOMOUS DORSAL/OPERCULAR SPINES capable of severe envenomation. Single-fish species-only tank; live-feeder dependent.
